findings.description: Heading styling and alignment differ noticeably from the source.
findings.evidence: On page 1, the PDF shows a centered serif heading block with smaller title text and multiline stacked organization lines. The HTML renders a much larger sans-serif title with heavier visual emphasis and a thick horizontal rule, changing the visual hierarchy though not the content.

findings.description: HTML contains a fabricated EPA ID number in the body text that does not match the PDF.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'Documents may be located using the EPA ID Number: NCD049773245.' HTML: 'Documents may be located using the EPA ID Number: NCD049773245.' This value is inconsistent with the document header EPA ID in the PDF ('NCD 045 646 924') and appears to be an invented/incorrect identifier within the generated content context.

findings.description: One sentence is altered in a way that changes meaning by omitting the noun after 'A reinforced concrete was installed.'
findings.evidence: PDF: 'A reinforced concrete was installed.' HTML repeats the same text, but the phrase appears incomplete relative to the source context about closure/remediation infrastructure; this suggests OCR/source ambiguity. More importantly, the conversion preserved a likely malformed sentence rather than a meaningful structure, reducing textual accuracy.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy is not an accurate representation of the document structure; the page header 'FACT SHEET' is not the document title, while the permit title is promoted to h1.
findings.evidence:
PDF visually shows 'FACT SHEET' as the top document heading, followed by centered permit title text. HTML uses '
FACT SHEET
' and '

Draft Corrective Action Permit for Kaba Ilco Corporation

'. This changes the structural hierarchy of the source.
